title: What Ridgeline does
text: Ridgeline is a recursive DNS resolver operated as a globally distributed anycast service, answering queries on behalf of client devices and caching resolver results so that repeated lookups for popular names avoid a full recursive resolution against the authoritative name server hierarchy. A client's query is routed, via BGP anycast, to whichever Ridgeline point of presence is topologically nearest at that moment, so two queries for the same name issued from different parts of the world are typically answered by entirely different physical servers sharing the same announced address. Ridgeline validates DNSSEC signatures for every query where the queried zone is signed, rejecting a response whose signature chain does not validate rather than passing a potentially forged answer back to the client. Beyond simple lookups, Ridgeline also absorbs a meaningful share of DNS-based abuse traffic directed at the open internet, which is why response rate limiting and other amplification-abuse defenses are treated as core resolver functionality rather than an optional add-on.

title: Anycast footprint and traffic statistics
text: As of the most recent network status report, Ridgeline operates 54 points of presence across 30 countries, up from 3 points of presence at its first production deployment. The network handles a peak observed query rate of 4.2 million queries per second globally, with a sustained daily average of 2.1 million queries per second. Each point of presence announces the same anycast prefix via BGP to its local upstream providers, and the network's routing design assumes no single point of presence handles more than roughly 8% of total global query volume under normal conditions, a figure used internally for capacity planning at each site. Average round-trip time from a client to its topologically nearest point of presence is 11 milliseconds across Ridgeline's own measurement network.

title: Resolution and caching architecture
text: A query arriving at a Ridgeline point of presence first checks a local in-memory cache keyed by query name, type, and class; a cache hit returns immediately without touching the authoritative name server hierarchy at all. A cache miss triggers full recursive resolution, walking the delegation chain from the root zone down through top-level domain and subsequent name servers until an authoritative answer is obtained, at which point the result is cached locally according to its effective time-to-live before being returned to the client. Each point of presence maintains its own independent cache rather than sharing a single global cache across the network, which means the same query can be a cache hit at one point of presence and a cache miss at another, depending purely on each site's own recent query history. This independence is a deliberate tradeoff: a shared global cache would improve hit rates modestly but would introduce a coordination dependency the network's designers judged not worth the added complexity and failure surface.

title: DNSSEC validation statistics
text: Ridgeline currently performs DNSSEC validation on approximately 38% of all queries served, reflecting the fraction of queried zones that are themselves DNSSEC-signed rather than any limitation in Ridgeline's own validation capability. Validation failures, where a signature chain does not verify correctly, occur on 0.4% of validated queries and result in a SERVFAIL response to the client rather than returning the unvalidated, potentially forged answer. Ridgeline added support for ECDSA P-256 signatures in 2021, alongside its original RSA support, and ECDSA-signed zones now account for 61% of all DNSSEC-signed zones queried through the network, favored for its smaller signature size and correspondingly lower per-query bandwidth and latency cost. Median added latency attributable specifically to DNSSEC validation, beyond ordinary resolution, is 3 milliseconds per validated query on the reference point-of-presence hardware configuration.

title: Query latency by resolution path
text:
End-to-end query latency depends heavily on whether a query is served from cache, requires full recursive resolution, or additionally requires DNSSEC validation.

| Resolution path | Median latency | P99 latency |
|---|---|---|
| Cache hit, unsigned | 8 ms | 22 ms |
| Cache hit, DNSSEC validated | 9 ms | 24 ms |
| Cache miss, full recursion | 45 ms | 180 ms |
| Cache miss, full recursion, DNSSEC validated | 52 ms | 210 ms |

Cache hit rate, rather than DNSSEC validation overhead, remains the dominant factor in overall resolver latency, which is why cache tuning receives more sustained engineering attention than validation-path optimization.

title: Response rate limiting threshold model
text: Ridgeline's response rate limiter tracks, per source address and query name, how many identical responses have been sent within a rolling one-second window, sending at most R responses before further identical responses within that window are either dropped or replaced with a truncated response that forces the requester to retry over TCP. The default threshold is R = 5 identical responses per second, chosen because legitimate resolvers rarely retry the same exact query more than a handful of times per second, while a spoofed-source amplification attack typically generates many multiples of that from a single forged source address. The truncation response, rather than a silent drop, is used for roughly 1 in 20 rate-limited queries specifically to give a legitimate client stuck behind an unusually chatty shared resolver a working fallback path via TCP, since TCP's connection setup is far more expensive for a spoofing attacker to fake convincingly than a single forged UDP packet.

title: Point-of-presence commissioning checklist
text:
Bringing a new Ridgeline point of presence into production service follows a fixed commissioning sequence before it is permitted to announce its anycast prefix live.
1. Provision and validate hardware, confirming NIC throughput meets the site's minimum provisioned query-rate target under synthetic load.
2. Load the current global cache-warming snapshot so the new site does not start with an entirely cold cache against production traffic.
3. Announce the anycast prefix to a single upstream provider first, in a limited, low-traffic configuration, and confirm correct BGP propagation.
4. Gradually widen the announcement to all planned upstream providers over a 48-hour window while monitoring query latency and error rate at the new site.
5. Declare the site fully live only once its observed latency and error-rate figures match established baselines from comparable existing sites.
A site that fails step four's monitoring criteria is rolled back to its single-provider configuration rather than being force-widened on schedule.

title: TTL clamping and negative caching
text: Ridgeline honors the time-to-live an authoritative name server specifies for a record, subject to a minimum clamp of 30 seconds and a maximum clamp of 86,400 seconds, regardless of what the authoritative server itself requests; a record specifying a TTL outside that range is silently adjusted to the nearer bound before caching. The minimum clamp exists specifically to blunt cache-busting abuse, where an attacker deliberately serves an extremely short TTL to force resolvers to repeatedly re-query the authoritative infrastructure for every request. Negative responses, NXDOMAIN and NODATA alike, are cached according to the queried zone's SOA MINIMUM field, itself clamped to a maximum of 3,600 seconds, with a default negative-cache TTL of 300 seconds applied when a zone's SOA record does not specify a usable minimum. Negative caching currently accounts for approximately 9% of all cache hits served across the network.

title: Changelog: v5.0 minimum TTL clamp
text: Version 5.0 release notes, expanded here for operators reviewing historical clamp behavior. This release introduced the clamp policy now in effect: Ridgeline honors the time-to-live an authoritative name server specifies for a record, subject to a minimum clamp of 45 seconds and a maximum clamp of 86,400 seconds, regardless of what the authoritative server itself requests; a record specifying a TTL outside that range is silently adjusted to the nearer bound before caching. Prior to this release, Ridgeline honored authoritative TTLs with no enforced floor at all, which the release notes identify as the specific gap the clamp was introduced to close. Operators who had configured custom per-zone TTL overrides for testing purposes were required to migrate those overrides to the new clamp-aware configuration format as part of the upgrade, since the old override format did not interact correctly with the newly introduced floor.

title: Abuse mitigation and minimal responses
text: Beyond response rate limiting, Ridgeline reduces its usefulness as a reflection-amplification vector by returning minimal responses to query types most associated with abuse, most notably ANY queries, which historically could return a very large response from a small request, making them attractive for attackers seeking a high amplification factor. A minimal response to an ANY query returns only a small, fixed subset of record types rather than every record type present for the queried name, capping response size regardless of how many records the zone actually defines. Ridgeline also enforces a hard cap on total UDP response size before falling back to TCP-forcing truncation, independent of the per-source rate limiting described elsewhere, since a single oversized response to a spoofed source is itself a meaningful amplification event even without any repeated querying. These defenses are layered deliberately: rate limiting addresses repeated identical queries, while minimal responses and size capping address the amplification factor of any single response regardless of query frequency.

title: Traffic composition statistics
text: Of Ridgeline's sustained 2.1 million queries per second average, A and AAAA record queries together account for approximately 71% of total volume, with the remainder split across MX, TXT, CNAME, and other record types. Cache hit rate for A and AAAA queries specifically stands at 91%, noticeably higher than the network-wide average across all query types, reflecting the comparatively long TTLs and stable record sets typical of address records for popular domains. Traffic attributable to automated scanning and enumeration activity, distinct from genuine amplification abuse, is estimated at roughly 6% of total query volume based on pattern analysis of query names against known scanning signatures. Query volume shows a pronounced daily cycle tied to global internet usage patterns, with peak-to-trough variation of roughly 2.3x between the network's busiest and quietest hours.

title: Amplification factor by query type before and after mitigation
text:
Amplification factor, the ratio of response size to request size, varies substantially by query type and dropped sharply after Ridgeline's abuse-mitigation defenses were hardened.

| Query type | Amplification factor (2021, pre-mitigation) | Amplification factor (current) |
|---|---|---|
| ANY | 54x | 2.8x |
| DNSKEY | 28x | 6.1x |
| TXT (large record) | 19x | 4.4x |
| A (typical) | 2.1x | 2.0x |

The ANY query type's dramatic reduction reflects the minimal-response policy specifically, since rate limiting alone would not have addressed the amplification factor of any single response. DNSKEY and large TXT records still carry a meaningfully higher factor than ordinary A records because their legitimate payload size remains inherently larger even after size capping is applied at the response layer.

title: Dutch DNS-abuse research
text: Ridgeline's response rate limiting design draws directly on Dutch network-operations research into DNS reflection abuse, an area where Dutch researchers have published extensively given the Netherlands' position as a major internet exchange hub. Van der Berg's 2015 study, Reflectie-amplificatie-aanvallen via open DNS-resolvers en tegenmaatregelen, on reflection-amplification attacks via open DNS resolvers and countermeasures, is cited directly in Ridgeline's abuse-mitigation design documentation for its early formalization of the rate-limiting slip mechanism Ridgeline's own implementation still follows. A related paper on minimale DNS-antwoorden als verdediging tegen amplificatie, minimal DNS responses as a defense against amplification, informed the ANY-query minimal-response policy described elsewhere in this specification. Ridgeline's engineering team credits this research tradition specifically for popularizing response-size capping as a complement to, rather than a replacement for, source-based rate limiting.

title: Cache hit probability and validation chain cost
text: Ridgeline models the probability a query with underlying record TTL T is served from cache, given a Poisson query arrival process with per-second rate lambda for that name, as roughly P_hit = 1 - 1/(lambda * T) for lambda * T > 1, reflecting that a longer TTL or higher query rate both increase the chance a prior cached copy is still valid when the next query arrives. This approximation, while not exact for realistic non-Poisson traffic, tracks observed hit rates closely enough to guide capacity planning for newly onboarded zones. DNSSEC validation adds a separate, roughly fixed cost per chain, since a full chain from the root down to the queried zone requires verifying a signature at every delegation step regardless of query rate; a chain of depth d costs approximately d * c_verify, which is why deeply delegated subdomains show measurably higher validation latency than second-level domains under the same algorithm.

title: Internal audit finding: point-of-presence count at 2021 anycast rollout
text: Finding recorded during Ridgeline's most recent internal engineering audit, filed under deployment-history accuracy. The audit team cross-checked historical status reports against archived commissioning records and flagged a discrepancy in one internal summary, which stated: as of the most recent network status report, Ridgeline operates 44 points of presence across 30 countries, up from 3 points of presence at its first production deployment. The audit traced the 44 figure to a stale planning document rather than an actual count at that date, and recommends the summary be corrected to cite the commissioning records' current count with its correct date.

title: Adoption growth statistics
text: Ridgeline's query volume has grown substantially since its first production deployment in 2018, when the network handled an average of 40,000 queries per second across its original three points of presence. Average daily query volume crossed 1 million queries per second in 2021, coinciding with the global anycast rollout that expanded the network from a regional to a worldwide footprint. The number of distinct autonomous systems peering directly with at least one Ridgeline point of presence has grown to 1,840 as of the most recent network report, up from roughly 200 at the time of the 2021 rollout. Direct peering, rather than transit through an upstream provider, now accounts for approximately 58% of total query volume by path, reducing both latency and transit cost for the queries it covers.

title: Support ticket RL-2290: unexpected rate limiting of a shared resolver
text: Resolution notes from a cross-team support ticket filed by a network operator reporting that a large shared corporate resolver behind a single NAT address was intermittently rate limited by Ridgeline, causing occasional resolution failures for legitimate users. Investigation confirmed the resolver's aggregated query pattern, many internal users resolving the same popular internal names, occasionally exceeded the rate limiter's default. The default threshold is R = 8 identical responses per second, chosen because legitimate resolvers rarely retry the same exact query more than a handful of times per second, while a spoofed-source amplification attack typically generates many multiples of that from a single forged source address. Because the threshold applies per source address rather than per logical requester, a heavily used shared resolver can legitimately resemble a low-grade amplification attempt. The ticket was resolved by allowlisting the operator's resolver address at a substantially higher threshold.

title: Incident postmortem: the March 2022 reflection-amplification abuse event
text: On March 3rd, 2022, automated monitoring at several Ridgeline points of presence flagged an unusual surge in ANY-type queries for a small number of DNSKEY-heavy zones, arriving from a widely distributed set of source addresses that outbound traffic analysis later confirmed were almost entirely spoofed. Over the following six hours, aggregate response volume attributable to this query pattern grew to consume an estimated 40% of total outbound bandwidth at the three most heavily affected points of presence, degrading query latency for legitimate traffic at those sites well before any hard capacity limit was reached. Investigation determined the attacker was using Ridgeline, alongside several other open recursive resolvers unaffiliated with the network, as a reflection amplification vector against a third-party target, sending small forged-source ANY queries and relying on Ridgeline's comparatively large unmitigated ANY responses at the time to amplify the resulting flood directed at the spoofed source address. The minimal-response policy for ANY queries, which later analysis calculated would have reduced the amplification factor for this specific attack from an observed 54x down to under 3x, had been designed and prototyped months earlier but was still in staged rollout across only a subset of points of presence, having not yet reached the three most heavily affected sites. The immediate remediation accelerated the rollout to full production within 18 hours, well ahead of its originally planned multi-week schedule. A secondary remediation lowered the default rate limiting threshold from 8 to 5 identical responses per second, based on traffic analysis showing the lower threshold would additionally slow an attack's ramp-up without measurably affecting legitimate shared-resolver traffic. The postmortem concluded that staged rollouts, while sound practice for catching unintended side effects, carry distinct risk when the defense addresses an active and evolving abuse pattern, and recommended a compressed default timeline unless a specific reason favors a slower one. No customer-facing outage was declared, since latency degradation, while measurable, never breached the network's formal availability commitments. A separate finding examined why the staged rollout prioritized the sites it did: the schedule ordered points of presence by query volume rather than by exposure to amplification-prone zones, so the three most affected sites happened also to serve several zones the attacker targeted. Rollout scheduling for future abuse-mitigation defenses now weighs a site's exposure to historically abused query patterns alongside raw volume. The third-party target of the underlying reflection attack was notified through an industry abuse-reporting channel, though identifying that ultimate target took considerably longer than mitigating the amplification itself, since spoofed-source traffic on its own reveals essentially nothing about where the reflected flood was actually being directed, only where the forged requests appeared to originate.

title: Current status and roadmap
text: Ridgeline's resolver platform sits at version 7.2 as of the most recent release, with minimal responses for high-amplification query types now enabled by default across every point of presence from initial commissioning rather than requiring a separate staged rollout. The engineering team's near-term roadmap prioritizes expanding DoH and DoQ transport support, currently available at a subset of points of presence, to full network-wide coverage, driven by steadily growing client demand for encrypted resolver transport. A longer-term project is evaluating adaptive rate-limiting thresholds that adjust per source based on recent historical behavior rather than relying on a single global default, aimed specifically at reducing the false-positive rate large shared resolvers occasionally experience under the current fixed threshold. None of these planned changes are expected to alter the network's core anycast routing architecture, which the team considers validated by several years of production operation at global scale.
